---
title: "Response to Reviewers"
author: "Carmen Cabrera and Francisco Rowe"
format: 
  pdf:
    pdf-engine: pdflatex
header-includes:
  - \usepackage{enumitem}
---





## Point-by-point response to reviewers

\section{Reviewer 1}

\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}This is a good paper that addresses some important issues with mobile phone data (MPD) in assessing the effects of shocks. I have a number of concerns that should be addressed before it is ready for publication, however.

\subsection{Motivation and base case}

\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{Reviewer 1, Comment 1 (R1.1).} First, at a meta level, I wonder about impact. Many researchers working with MPD will have already shown population-coverage correlations and looked at demographic biases in deviations/residuals. Because many reviewers/researchers will address bias in the SI of their papers, I think the study would benefit from more work on whether or not the typical strategies are insufficient. This could be as simple as using a linear model, residualising coverage on population and looking at demographic/spatial biases in the residuals. I think the strength of this study actually lies in the spatial dimension, since most data validations will not include checks for spatial autocorrelation on the residuals of this coverage  population model, but these are important as diagnostics that indicate unmodelled spatial structure, whether or not there might be some sort of unobserved/unmodelled bias in the data. Building this “base case” and finding out if it is insufficient, then, represents an important first step in improving the paper.}

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{Authors response (A):} We agree that many studies working with mobile phone data (MPD) assess, to some extents, data quality. Typically, this is done by examining correlations between platform-derived population counts and census population counts [and sometimes by analysing demographic patterns in residuals from simple linear models]. These approaches provide a useful first-order diagnostic of proportional agreement.

However, as the reviewer notes, it is important to establish whether such standard validation strategies are sufficient. In response, we have now strengthened the manuscript by explicitly discussing this ``base case'' validation strategy and clarifying its limitations. In particular, we highlight that:

\begin{itemize}[noitemsep, topsep=0pt]
\item High correlations between MPD-derived and census population counts are often driven by shared scaling with area population size and therefore do not necessarily imply representativeness. 
\item Assessment strategies based on linear regression, assume a linear relationship between bias and population size, and typically do not test for spatial autocorrelation in residuals. Spatially structured or nonlinear patterns of bias may therefore remain undetected under conventional approaches.
\end{itemize}

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} We have revised the Introduction and Methods sections to clarify ... Specifically, our approach formalises coverage bias as an explicit proportional metric, diagnoses its spatial structure through Moran's I statistics, and identifies multidimensional and nonlinear contextual drivers using explainable machine learning. By explicitly evaluating spatial dependence and nonlinear effects, our framework provides a more systematic and diagnostic assessment of representativeness than correlation-based validation alone.}

\subsection{Bias measure}

\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R1.2}: The authors present a measure of bias $e_i$ that represents the deviations from perfect coverage They suggest that a situation wherein there more mobile devices that people within a given areal unit, such that $1 - c_i < 0$, is rare, so the measure functionally bounded [0, 1]. Because it could happen, though, a mention of how it is handled is also important—eg winsorisation/censoring.}

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} The reviewer is correct that our definition of coverage $c_i = P_i^D/P_i \times 100$ allows $c_i>100$ when the observed number of devices or users exceeds the census population in an area, which would yield negative values of our bias measure $e_i = 100-c_i$. While this situation is theoretically possible (e.g. due to device duplication, temporal mismatch or other measurement artefacts), it is far from arising in our empirical application. We therefore do not need to apply winsorisation or censoring. In principle, allowing $e_i$ to take negative values is methodologically acceptable and interpretable as ``over-coverage'' relative to the census benchmark. Our modelling approach can accommodate such values without difficulty.


\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} TO DO}


\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R1.3}: Another problem is that the bias might be subtler than that because coverage is often low that even devices doubled or tripled in some households would still not push $c_i > 1$: that is, because these samples are often on the order 10\% of the population, there could still be many duplicates per household. I do not think there is a good solution here, and likely only exists for MPD, not for Facebook/Twitter, but I think the authors should comment on it and include a check for devices that display X\% similarity. A complete process for finding duplicates is beyond the scope of the paper but it could be an important source of bias so I also do not think we can write it off out of hand; some parsimonious check would help.}

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} We agree that multi-device ownership or duplication is a potential source of inflation in device-based population counts. In principle, individuals may own multiple devices or generate multiple identifiers and such duplication could introduce systematic overestimation in certain areas.

Conceptually, our framework measures cumulative population coverage bias, defined as the deviation between platform-derived counts and census population counts. Any inflation arising from ownership of more than one device is therefore captured within the coverage bias metric. In other words, we do not assume that there is no duplication but is treated as one potential component of the overall bias we seek to quantify.

Regarding household-level duplication specifically, our unit of inference is the individual population count rather than households. Duplication would only constitute a systematic concern if multi-device ownership varies spatially in ways correlated with area-level characteristics (e.g. income, level of urbanisation). In such cases, our explainable machine learning framework is designed precisely to detect these contextual associations. For example, if multi-device ownership is more prevalent in affluent or highly urbanised areas, this would be reflected in the importance of socioeconomic or geographic predictors in explaining coverage bias.

We acknowledge, however, that a complete identification of duplicate devices would require access to individual-level or cross-device linkage data, which is not available in aggregated and privacy-preserving datasets. Generalisability is a key principle of our proposed methodology, therefore... 

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} TO DO}


\subsection{Model}

\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R1.4}: Given that the paper explores potential spatial biases in the data, the k-fold cross-validation should include spatial folds as well; k-fold with contiguity clusters would help and something with regions (like leaving London out) would be even stronger.}

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} This is an important methodological suggestion that we have now implemented. In response, we have implemented additional spatially structured validation procedures as robustness checks.</p>


\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} We have implemented additional spatially structured validation procedures as robustness checks. Specifically:}

\begin{itemize}[noitemsep, topsep=0pt]
\item We conducted spatial block cross-validation, where folds were defined using geographically contiguous clusters of Local Authority Districts to reduce spatial leakage between training and testing samples. --*TO DO*
\item We implemented a leave-one-region-out validation scheme based on NUTS1 regions (including a hold-out of London), training the model on all remaining regions and testing on the excluded region.--*TO DO*
\end{itemize}

Across these spatial validation schemes, model performance metrics were comparable to .... (?) those obtained under standard k-fold cross-validation, and the ranking of feature importance remained substantively stable. These results confirm our earlier finding that spatial autocorrelation in coverage bias is weak at the LAD scale and does not impact inference (?) --> Update from results.


\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R1.5}: The authors also opt out of using spatial lags in the model, because there is not strong autocorrelation in the DV, but this is probably the wrong call: a lack of autocorrelation in the DV does not guarantee that the residuals are not autocorrelated. Further, because this approach is automated, using regularisation to determine what makes the cut, it makes more sense to include lags (of predictors) and let ML do the work of figuring out if they are important. Please also report Moran’s I of residuals.}

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} We agree that weak spatial autocorrelation in the dependent variable does not rule out spatial dependence in model errors. We incorporate the suggestions given by the reviewer in our methodology and based on these additional tests, we find that residual spatial autocorrelation is negligible (?) at the LAD scale and inclusion of spatially lagged predictors does not change model performance or the conclusions regarding the leading contextual drivers of population coverage bias. --TO UPDATE

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} We have revised the manuscript to clarify this distinction and to avoid implying that the small magnitude of Moran’s I in the outcome is sufficient evidence that spatial effects can be ignored.--TODO}

\begin{itemize}
\item We now report Moran’s I for the model residuals for each data source, using the same spatial weighting scheme(s) considered in the paper. This provides a diagnostic of any remaining unmodelled spatial structure after accounting for the covariates. -- TODO
\item We augmented the set of covaraites with spatially lagged versions of key covariates (constructed using contiguity-based spatial weights) and re-estimated the XGBoost models.--TODO
\item We report these results in Section X and provide full details in Supplementary Material Section Y --TODO.
\end{itemize}


\subsection{Less important}

\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R1.6}: There is no mention of how many LADs are in England but it seems small. XGBoost is fit for purpose here, but including hyperparameters to alleviate any concerns about overfitting would be good. Please report capacity controls; add a brief calibration plot (predicted vs. observed) and residual maps.}

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} We have revised the manuscript in several ways:}
\begin{itemize}
\item First, we now explicitly state the number of LADs analysed to clarify the scale of inference. 
\item Second, we have expanded the Methods section to report the key XGBoost hyperparameters used to control model capacity, including maximum tree depth, learning rate (eta), subsample ratio, column subsampling, and L1 and L2 regularisation penalties. These parameters were tuned using cross-validation and selected based on minimising out-of-sample RMSE. We also clarify that regularisation and early stopping criteria were used to prevent excessive tree growth and mitigate overfitting. 
\item Third, we have added a calibration plot (predicted versus observed coverage bias) to assess model fit and identify any systematic deviations. 
\item Finally, we include maps of model residuals for each data source to provide a visual diagnostic of remaining spatial structure, complementing the reported Moran’s I statistics of residuals.
\end{itemize}


\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R1.7}: Figure 4 looks great but ``Lower supervisory'' should be put either at noon or 6 (as it were, on the clock) so that it doesn’t protrude so far off to the side. As it is, that text pushes all the other figures off to the left side.

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} We have now revised the Figure according to the reviewer's suggestions.}

\section{Reviewer 2}

The paper presents a national-scale assessment of bias in social media and multi-source mobile phone application data by comparing these sources to the census, then linking patterns to area characteristics using an interpretable machine-learning approach. The study is ambitious, the analysis appears robust, and the figures and overall presentation are mostly strong. A revision that clarifies a few key points would strengthen the paper and make the contribution easier to interpret and reuse.

\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{Reviewer 2, Comment 1 (R2.1):} At the moment, the paper reads more strongly as a set of findings for these specific data sources in the UK than as a general workflow. This is mainly because the home-area approach builds on existing methods and the analysis is then carried out at Local Authority District (LAD) level, which is higher than the scale many studies would use to link census/IMD variables or to adjust for bias. It may help to either frame the contribution more clearly as UK-wide evidence, or explain more directly how and why the approach would transfer to other places and finer geographic scales.}


\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} We would like to clarify that the contribution of this paper is methodological, since our intention is to propose a generalisable framework for assessing population coverage bias in mobile phone application data. The framework is illustrated through a UK-wide application.

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} To make this clearer we have taken two actions:}

\begin{itemize}
\item We have revised the Introduction and Discussion to clarify the methodological contribution more explicitly. We now emphasise that the proposed workflow comprises three stages: i) formalised measurement of coverage bias, ii) spatial diagnostic analysis, and iii) explainable machine learning to identify contextual drivers. We provide details that the workflow are not specific to the UK context or to Local Authority District geography. Rather, it is designed to be adaptable to any setting where (a) an external population benchmark is available and (b) area-level covariates can be linked to digital trace data.
\item We also clarify why LADs were selected for this empirical application. The choice reflects data availability constraints (e.g. Facebook and Twitter/X aggregation levels) and the need for comparability across multiple providers. The framework itself is scale-flexible and can be implemented at finer spatial resolutions (e.g. MSOA or grid-cell level) if data is accessible. We have added text to make this distinction explicit and to discuss how the approach could transfer to other national contexts, including settings with different census structures or where gridded population datasets (e.g. WorldPop) serve as benchmarks.
\end{itemize}


\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R2.2:} The choice to work at LAD level needs a clearer justification and a fuller discussion of what it means for interpretation. LADs are useful for a national overview, but many common uses of these data (including mobility analysis) rely on smaller areas. The paper notes that results may differ at other geographies (page 14, line 9); expanding this would help readers understand whether the LAD patterns are likely to hold when broken down into smaller areas. The home-area workflow also needs a little more detail: the paper should state the geography at which home area is first estimated (before aggregation to LAD), whether the same method is used for multi-source dataset 1 and dataset 2, and what ``privacy preserving'' means in practice for this study.}


\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} We agree that the choice of spatial scale and the home-area estimation procedure require clearer justification and fuller explanation. Our intention is to present a generalisable workflow illustrated through a UK-wide application; however, we recognise that the manuscript could more clearly distinguish between methodological contribution and empirical scale-specific findings.

Regarding the use of Local Authority Districts (LADs), we selected this geography primarily to ensure comparability across data sources, as some providers (e.g. Facebook and Twitter/X) release data only at relatively coarse aggregated levels. LADs provide a harmonised spatial framework for cross-platform benchmarking at national scale. We acknowledge, however, that many applications of mobile phone data operate at finer spatial resolutions. Bias patterns may differ at smaller geographies due to within-area heterogeneity and the Modifiable Areal Unit Problem (MAUP). The proposed workflow is not inherently tied to the LAD scale and can be implemented at finer resolutions (e.g, MSOA or grid level) where data availability permits. We agree that this transferability should be more clearly articulated.

We also agree that the home-area estimation procedure and the meaning of “privacy-preserving” require greater transparency. While the home-detection approach builds on established methods, it is important to clarify the initial geography of home assignment, whether methods differ across datasets, and how privacy constraints shape the accessible data.

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} We have revised the manuscript in the following ways:}

\begin{itemize}
\item We have expanded the Methods section to explicitly justify the use of LADs as a harmonised national unit driven by cross-provider comparability and data access constraints.
\item We have strengthened the Discussion to explain how bias patterns may differ at finer geographic scales and to acknowledge potential MAUP effects. We clarify that the proposed workflow is scale-flexible and can be implemented at smaller spatial units where data access allows.
\item We now explicitly state the spatial resolution at which home locations are first estimated before aggregation to LADs. We clarify that a consistent nighttime-frequency rule is applied to both multi-app datasets, while Facebook and Twitter/X rely on provider-defined home assignments.
\item We have added detail explaining that all analyses are conducted on anonymised, aggregated data; that no individual-level identifiers are accessible; and that provider-side safeguards (e.g. small-count suppression, noise injection, spatial smoothing where applicable) are applied prior to data access.}


\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R2.3:} Population coverage measures should be defined more clearly. In particular, it should be stated whether the denominator is the adult population only, given that GDPR mobile app products typically cover users aged 16+. If children are included in the denominator, that should be acknowledged as a limitation. It would also be useful to note how differences in the adult share across areas might affect equation 3.1 and later steps that use ``population coverage bias''.}


\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} In our analysis, the denominator corresponds to the total resident population (all ages) as reported in the 2021 Census. We acknowledge that, in principle, if mobile application datasets disproportionately represent individuals aged 16+, including children in the denominator could mechanically increase the estimated coverage bias in areas with a larger share of young people.

However, we note that we do not observe individual-level age information in the digital trace datasets and therefore cannot verify that all captured traces correspond exclusively to individuals aged 16+. In practice, some devices or application accounts may be associated with younger users. As such, redefining the denominator as the 16+ population would impose an assumption that cannot be empirically validated with the available data. For transparency and comparability across sources, we therefore retain the total resident population as the benchmark.

We further agree that spatial variation in age structure may influence Equation (3.1) and subsequent modelling stages. Importantly, age composition variables are included among the area-level predictors in the explainable machine learning framework, allowing the model to detect and quantify whether differences in the adult share systematically contribute to observed coverage bias.

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} We have taken the following actions:}

\begin{itemize}
\item We have clarified in the Methods section that the denominator in the population coverage calculation corresponds to the total resident population (all ages) from the 2021 Census.
\item We have added text acknowledging that many mobile application datasets primarily target users aged 16+, and that inclusion of children in the denominator may influence coverage estimates.
\item  We now explicitly state that, because age is unobserved in the digital trace data, we cannot verify whether all captured users are over 16, and therefore cannot reliably restrict the denominator to the adult population.
\item We have clarified that age structure is included among the explanatory covariates in the modelling stage, allowing the framework to assess whether demographic composition drives spatial variation in coverage bias.
\end{itemize}

\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R2.4:} In a few places, the text would benefit from clearer separation between coverage, bias, and representativeness. Higher penetration for Facebook and X does not necessarily mean these sources are representative, and multi-source app data may have lower coverage but still be more balanced across some groups (as suggested in prior UK evidence). This distinction is mentioned in the discussion, but it should be applied more consistently in the Results and Discussion, especially in section 4b where social media show higher coverage.}


\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} We agree: higher coverage does not imply representativeness. A source may achieve relatively high penetration while still systematically over- or under-representing particular demographic or socioeconomic groups. Conversely, a source with lower overall coverage may provide a more balanced representation across certain subpopulations.


\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} In response:}
\begin{itemize}
\item We have revised the Results and Discussion sections to more consistently distinguish between (i) overall coverage levels, (ii) population coverage bias as defined in Equation (3.1), and (iii) representativeness across demographic and socioeconomic groups.
\item In Section 4b, we have amended the language to clarify that higher coverage observed in Facebook and Twitter/X reflects greater penetration, but does not imply that these sources are more representative across all groups or geographies.
\item We have strengthened the Discussion to explicitly state that representativeness is multidimensional and cannot be inferred from coverage magnitude alone. We clarify that our explainable machine learning framework is designed to identify patterns of over- and under-representation independent of overall penetration.
\end{itemize}


\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R2.5:} Some figure captions and labels could be made clearer. Figure 1 is difficult to interpret due to the dual axis and unclear mapping between axes and legend. In Figure 3, ``population coverage bias'' appears to be on a 0-100 scale, but the wording (``size of the bias'') suggests higher values are worse. If values closer to 100 indicate higher coverage, the terminology and caption should make the direction of interpretation explicit. Relatedly, page 11, line 55 states that mobile phone app data have strong potential for large-scale empirical analysis; this would read better with a short qualification that coverage/volume alone do not resolve known measurement issues linked to how these data are collected and processed.}

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} We have taken the following actions:}
\begin{itemize}
\item We have revised Figure 1 and 3.
\item On page 11, line 55, we have revised the statement regarding the potential of mobile phone app data for large-scale empirical analysis.
\end{itemize}


\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R2.6:}  The reported coverage levels for multi-source app data also need clearer support. The paper refers to high coverage values (e.g., 70\% and 26\% for an undisclosed provider), which appear high compared with common estimates for these datasets (often single-digit percentages or around 10\%, depending on definition and time window). The paper should state exactly what these percentages represent (time window, unique active users, de-duplication, denominator) and provide evidence or careful qualification, particularly for the 26\% figure.}


\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} Typo??

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} TO DO}


\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R2.7:} Processing choices for Twitter/X should be described. Twitter geolocation can differ between GPS geotags and platform-assigned locations, and these can conflict; the paper should state how this was handled because it affects any bias/coverage comparisons for X/Twitter.}

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} In our analysis, we rely on the processed dataset in which home locations are inferred using a frequency-based approach, drawing on geolocated tweets derived from GPS tags when they were available or API-provided bounding boxes. EXPLAIN more why LAD level Home-assignment procedure described in the referenced methodology (Wang et al., 2022), which assigns a user’s home to the most frequently observed location over the study period.

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} We have taken the following actions:}
\begin{itemize}
\item We have expanded the Twitter/X data description in the Data section to explicitly state the types of geolocation signals used (e.g., GPS geotags, API bounding boxes), the priority rules applied when multiple location types were available, how conflicting location information was handled during home assignment. --> REVISE
\item We have clarified in X section that the home-detection procedure follows the frequency-based method described in Wang et al. (2022), and that inferred home locations are subsequently aggregated to LAD level for comparability with other sources.
\end{itemize }


\setlength{\leftskip}{0cm}\textit{\textbf{R2.8:} The interpretation of results would be stronger with more engagement with related studies, particularly in the introduction and discussion, for example: 
\begin{itemize}
\item <https://doi.org/10.1038/s41597-024-03490-y>  \item<https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apgeog.2023.102997>  \item<https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apgeog.2024.103260>  \item<https://doi.org/10.1140/epjds/s13688-023-00447-w>  \item<https://doi.org/10.1111/geoj.70033>
\item<https://doi.org/10.3390/app15020982> \item<https://doi.org/10.1371/journal.pone.0294430>
\end{itemize}

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} We agree that engaging more deeply with this literature strengthens the positioning of our work and situates our findings within the broader field. In the revised manuscript, we have incorporated discussion of these studies where they align with our conceptual framing and empirical results. This has allowed us to ...

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} We have taken the following actions:}

\begin{itemize}
\item We have expanded the Introduction to cite and briefly summarise these recent contributions, emphasising how they relate to (a) the need for systematic bias assessment, and (b) the limitations of existing validation practices that focus primarily on correlations or single-source analyses.
\item We have integrated more detailed engagement with these studies in the Discussion, drawing clear connections between our findings and insights from the suggested literature, particularly in relation to (REVISE!) spatial heterogeneity in bias and coverage patterns, methodological innovations for bias diagnostics and the implications of digital divide and demographic composition for representativeness.
\item We point out how our results align with or extend findings from these studies — for example, in demonstrating the complexity of nonlinearity in bias drivers, and in showing that multi-app data may not automatically reduce coverage bias despite higher overall penetration.
\item All suggested references have been added to the bibliography and are appropriately connected to specific claims or components of the framework (e.g., measurement, spatial clustering, explainable modelling).
\end{itemize}


**R2.9**. The ethics section could also be clearer in plain terms, ideally within the paper rather than only as a brief statement. The current wording says approval was not required because no personal identifiers were processed and that the work followed University of Liverpool ethical standards. Given the use of Twitter Academic API data, estimation of home area from high-resolution traces, and processing of at least one multi-source dataset, the paper should briefly describe the safeguards in place (secure access, aggregation/suppression rules, limits on outputs) and whether the work was reviewed or formally exempted, noting that re-identification risk can still exist even when data are de-identified.


\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} All analyses were conducted under institutional data governance standards at the University of Liverpool. The datasets accessed were either: i) publicly available in processed and aggregated form (the dataset derived from the Twitter Academic API, the dataset from Locomizer and the one from Meta), or ii) provided under formal data-sharing agreements that restrict access to pseudo-anonymised records and prohibit attempts at re-identification. For case i) individual-level identifiers were not accessible to the research team, and in the case of Meta provider-side privacy protections (e.g., small-count suppression, thresholding, spatial smoothing, or pre-processed home assignment) were applied before data access where relevant. For case ii) outputs were generated only at aggregated geographic levels (LAD or higher) to avoid reidentification, and we report only area-level summaries consistent with privacy-preserving practice.

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} With the aim of clarifying the ethical standards followed in our work, we have}:
\begin{itemize}
\item We have revised the Ethics section to describe in more detail the governance framework under which the research was conducted, including compliance with University of Liverpool ethical standards.
\item We now explicitly state that: i) no personal identifiers were accessible to the researchers, ii) data were anonymised and analysed only in aggregated form, iii) data-sharing agreements restricted re-identification attempts and iv) outputs are reported at aggregate spatiotemporal scales only.
\end{itemize}


**R2.0**. Finally, the data/code availability statement should be clearer and aligned with journal requirements. The manuscript points to GitHub, but it is unclear whether data from Locomizer and the undisclosed provider will be shared (and Table 1 suggests they may not). Given TOP standards (Data transparency Level 2: data posted to a trusted repository; exceptions identified at submission), the paper should state what will be publicly available and where. If the underlying data cannot be shared due to licensing, it should state if anything be shared instead (e.g., aggregated outputs or a reproducible pipeline for authorised users) and how any exception was handled at submission.


\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textbf{A:} In this study, not all datasets can be made publicly available due to provider licensing agreements and non-disclosure constraints (including raw data from the undisclosed multi-source provider and from Meta). However, we recognise the importance of transparency and reproducibility under TOP guidelines. To address this, we clarify what materials are publicly accessible and what restrictions apply. 

All analysis code used to process, model and visualise the data is openly available via GitHub. Where data cannot be shared, we provide documentation describing data structure, preprocessing steps, and required inputs so that authorised users with legitimate access to equivalent datasets can reproduce the workflow. Where permissible, we indicate whether aggregated outputs or derived summary statistics can be shared.

We also clarify how these data availability constraints were declared at submission in accordance with journal policy, and that the limitations arise solely from third-party licensing agreements rather than from researcher-imposed restrictions.

\setlength{\leftskip}{1cm}\textcolor{blue}{\textbf{Action:} TO DO}


\section{References}
